epigraph.text: Trust in the Lord with all your heart, and do not lean on your own understanding.
epigraph.quote: In the long run, he who updates his beliefs with Bayes' rule cannot go far wrong.

content.text:
One of the most powerful tools in probability is the ability to 
*reverse* conditional probabilities—to go from $P(B \mid A)$ to 
$P(A \mid B)$. This is Bayes' Theorem.

Often we know $P(A \mid B)$ but want $P(B \mid A)$. Medical testing 
provides a classic example: we typically know the sensitivity 
$P(\text{positive} \mid \text{disease})$, but patients want to know 
$P(\text{disease} \mid \text{positive})$.

content.title: Bayes' Theorem
content.content:
For events $A$ and $B$ with $P(B) > 0$:
\[
 P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}
\]
More generally, if $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_k$ partition the sample space:
\[
 P(A_i \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{k} P(B \mid A_j) \cdot P(A_j)}
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
From the definition of conditional probability:
\[
 P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}
\]
By the multiplication rule, $P(A \cap B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$.
Substituting gives the result.

content.title: Thomas Bayes (1702--1761)
content.content:
Reverend Thomas Bayes was an English Presbyterian minister and 
mathematician. His famous theorem was published posthumously in 
1763, edited by his friend Richard Price. Bayes sought to solve 
the "inverse probability" problem: given observed outcomes, what 
can we infer about underlying causes? His work laid the foundation 
for Bayesian inference, which has become increasingly influential 
in modern statistics.

content.text:
A common error is to ignore the *prior probability* (base rate) 
when interpreting conditional probabilities.

content.title: Medical Testing Revisited
content.problem:
A disease affects 1\% of a population. A test has 95\% sensitivity 
and 90\% specificity. If a person tests positive, what is the 
probability they have the disease?
content.solution:
Let $D$ = "disease" and $+$ = "positive test."

Given: $P(D) = 0.01$, $P(+ \mid D) = 0.95$, $P(+ \mid D^c) = 0.10$.

We want $P(D \mid +)$.

First, find $P(+)$ using the law of total probability:
\[
 P(+) = P(D) \cdot P(+ \mid D) + P(D^c) \cdot P(+ \mid D^c)
 = (0.01)(0.95) + (0.99)(0.10) = 0.1085
\]

Now apply Bayes' theorem:
\[
 P(D \mid +) = \frac{P(+ \mid D) \cdot P(D)}{P(+)} 
 = \frac{(0.95)(0.01)}{0.1085} 
 = \frac{0.0095}{0.1085} 
 \approx 0.0876
\]

Only about 8.8\% of positive tests actually have the disease!

content.text:
This surprising result illustrates the **base rate fallacy**: 
when the disease is rare ($P(D) = 0.01$), even a good test produces 
many false positives because the healthy population is so large.

content.title: Don't Neglect Prior Probabilities
content.content:
The probability that a hypothesis is true given evidence depends not 
only on how likely the evidence is under that hypothesis, but also on 
how likely the hypothesis was *before* seeing the evidence.

content.text:
Bayes' theorem can be applied repeatedly as new evidence arrives, 
updating our probability estimates. The posterior probability from 
one application becomes the prior probability for the next.

content.title: Updating with New Evidence
content.problem:
In the medical testing example, suppose a person who tested positive 
takes a second, independent test and also tests positive. What is 
the new probability of disease?
content.solution:
After the first positive test, $P(D) = 0.0876$ (our new prior).

For the second test:
\[
 P(+_2) = P(D) \cdot P(+ \mid D) + P(D^c) \cdot P(+ \mid D^c)
 = (0.0876)(0.95) + (0.9124)(0.10) 
 \approx 0.1744
\]

Applying Bayes' theorem again:
\[
 P(D \mid +_2) = \frac{(0.95)(0.0876)}{0.1744} \approx 0.477
\]

After two positive tests, the probability of disease is about 48\%.

content.text:
Bayes' theorem formalizes a natural way of reasoning: we start with 
prior beliefs, gather evidence, and update our beliefs accordingly. 
This framework—prior $\to$ evidence $\to$ posterior—has profound 
implications for how we learn from data.

marginNotes.text:
If any of you lacks wisdom, let him ask God, 
who gives generously to all without reproach, and it will be given him.
